static_content_sections.title: About OceanPath Logistics
static_content_sections.content: OceanPath Logistics is a digital-first freight forwarder specializing in ocean shipping, inland trucking, and warehousing services. Our platform connects shippers with trusted carriers on major global trade lanes, providing instant quotes, live tracking, and integrated documentation tools for businesses of all sizes.

static_content_sections.title: Contact Information
static_content_sections.content: For sales and general inquiries, email sales@oceanpath.example or call +1 (312) 555-0199 (Mon–Fri, 08:00–18:00 CST). Our headquarters is located at 200 North Riverside Plaza, Chicago, IL 60606, USA.

static_content_sections.title: Privacy Policy & Terms of Service
static_content_sections.content: We collect and process shipment, contact, and billing data solely to provide logistics services and improve our platform. By using this website, you agree to our data processing practices, limitations of liability, and acceptable use rules. Key terms include: (1) you retain ownership of your data; (2) we act as a processor for shipment-related personal data; (3) service level commitments are outlined for quoting, booking, and tracking features; and (4) dispute resolution is handled under the laws of the State of Illinois, USA.

trade_lanes.name: Asia to Europe
trade_lanes.description: Primary east–west trade lane covering exports from major Asian ports to North, West, and South Europe.

trade_lanes.name: Asia to North America
trade_lanes.description: High-volume lane for shipments from Asia to the US and Canada West/East Coasts.

trade_lanes.name: Europe to North America
trade_lanes.description: Transatlantic services from North and South Europe to the US and Canada.

trade_lanes.name: Intra-Asia
trade_lanes.description: Regional services connecting ports within East, Southeast, and South Asia.

trade_lanes.name: Other Global Lanes
trade_lanes.description: All other combinations, including Latin America, Africa, and Oceania routes.

help_articles.title: Export documentation for machinery from USA to Brazil
help_articles.content: This guide outlines the documentation required to export industrial machinery from the United States to Brazil. Core documents include: commercial invoice, packing list, bill of lading, certificate of origin (preferably with any applicable trade agreement references), and insurance certificate. For Brazil, an Import Declaration (DI/DUIMP) must be pre-registered by the consignee, and the NCM classification for the machinery must be correctly assigned. Exporters in the U.S. must comply with AES/EEI filing rules if the value per Schedule B exceeds the reporting threshold. Additional documents can include technical catalogs, machine serial number lists, and, when applicable, ANVISA/INMETRO or other technical conformity certificates. Always confirm whether the shipment qualifies as temporary or permanent export and verify whether an agency or power of attorney is required for your customs broker in both countries.

help_articles.title: Export documentation checklist from USA to the European Union
help_articles.content: This checklist covers the standard export documentation flow from the United States to EU member states for general cargo. It explains how to prepare a compliant commercial invoice, packing list, and bill of lading, and when to obtain a certificate of origin or EUR.1/other preference forms. It also outlines EEI filing requirements in AES, screening against denied party lists, and common import documents requested in Europe such as the Single Administrative Document (SAD). The article includes sample templates and a step-by-step walkthrough of how these documents appear in our platform.

help_articles.title: Brazilian import customs process for industrial machinery
help_articles.content: This article explains the Brazilian customs clearance workflow for industrial machinery. It covers the role of the local customs broker, the use of the SISCOMEX system, and differences between temporary and permanent imports. It details how NCM tariff classification affects duty and tax rates, and what technical documentation (manuals, serial numbers, technical drawings) may be requested by Receita Federal. It also outlines common reasons for inspections and how to minimize clearance delays by pre-validating documents before shipment.

help_articles.title: IMDG Code basics for hazardous materials shipped by sea
help_articles.content: Learn how the IMDG Code applies to hazardous materials shipped by ocean. This overview explains UN numbers, proper shipping names, packing groups, and segregation rules. It also covers the dangerous goods declaration (DGD), shipper responsibilities, and common documentation errors that lead to rejections at the terminal.

help_articles.title: Vehicle export title and ownership checklist
help_articles.content: This guide lists the documents required to export vehicles by sea, including original title, bill of sale, and notarized power of attorney where applicable. It explains how U.S. Customs and Border Protection reviews title documents, typical inspection timelines at the port, and special considerations for salvage or rebuilt vehicles.

help_articles.title: LCL vs FCL: choosing the right ocean shipping mode
help_articles.content: This article compares Less-than-Container Load (LCL) and Full Container Load (FCL) services. It explains how pricing, transit time, handling risk, and cargo readiness affect your choice. Practical examples show when 3 pallets from Hamburg to New York make more sense as LCL versus upgrading to a 20' FCL, and how our quote tool calculates thresholds.

help_articles.title: How to read vessel schedules and voyage details
help_articles.content: Understand the structure of a vessel schedule: service name, voyage number, cut-off dates, ETD, ETA, and transshipment ports. This guide walks through a Rotterdam–Singapore example, explains what a hub-and-spoke service looks like, and shows how to interpret transshipment windows and potential delay risks.

help_articles.title: Incoterms 2020 overview for ocean shipments
help_articles.content: This overview explains the most commonly used Incoterms 2020 for ocean freight (FCA, FOB, CFR, CIF, DAP, DDP). It clarifies which party is responsible for freight, insurance, and customs, using examples such as CIF Shanghai–Los Angeles and FOB Hamburg. The article also explains how to capture Incoterms correctly on our booking flow.

help_articles.title: Creating a business profile and setting default shipping preferences
help_articles.content: This guide shows how to register a business account, add your company details, and configure default preferences. It includes step-by-step instructions for setting Asia-to-Europe as your preferred trade lane, choosing 40' Standard (Dry) as the default container type, and enabling email notifications for quotes and shipment updates.

help_articles.title: Billing cycles, payment methods, and credit terms
help_articles.content: Learn how invoices are generated for bookings, what payment methods we support, and how to request a credit line. The article explains pro-forma vs final invoices, how accessorial charges are billed, and where to download PDF copies within your dashboard.

help_articles.title: Customs clearance for electronics from China to the USA
help_articles.content: This article outlines typical documentation and compliance checks for importing electronics from China into the United States. It covers tariff classification under the HTSUS, Section 301 duties, FCC and FDA requirements for certain devices, and strategies to avoid holds and exams at U.S. ports.

help_articles.title: Packing guidelines for industrial equipment and machinery
help_articles.content: Proper packing reduces damage risk for heavy industrial equipment. This guide explains how to select crates, skids, and bracing, when to use vacuum packing or anti-corrosion coatings, and how to design lift points for safe handling. It also provides sample packing photos and a checklist you can share with your warehouse.

help_articles.title: Door-to-port trucking requirements in the United States
help_articles.content: This article explains what information is required to schedule a door pickup for containers in the U.S., including complete pickup address, ZIP code, contact details, and preferred time windows. It also outlines typical restrictions for 40' containers in dense urban areas and how chassis and detention fees work.

help_articles.title: Warehousing and storage fees explained
help_articles.content: Understand how warehousing fees are calculated based on pallet count, storage days, handling, and value-added services. This guide compares example quotes from Antwerp and Valencia for 10 pallets over 60 days and shows how our calculator estimates total storage cost before you submit a quote request.
